variants.treatment_strategies: When Li-Fraumeni syndrome is confirmed, a whole-body cancer surveillance protocol (Toronto protocol) is recommended, including annual whole-body MRI, breast MRI (for females), brain MRI, and abdominal ultrasound. Radiation exposure should be minimized, as radiation therapy carries a risk of secondary tumor induction and should be avoided when possible. Surgical resection is the primary treatment for cancer occurrence; responsiveness to platinum-based chemotherapy and immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab) is evaluated individually. Cascade genetic testing of family members is essential, and preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) may be considered for germline variant carriers.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The population frequency of pathogenic TP53 germline variants is estimated at approximately 1/5,000 to 1/20,000. The cumulative cancer incidence by age 70 in Li-Fraumeni syndrome patients reaches approximately 73% in males and approximately 100% in females. Large-scale studies on the exact frequency of TP53 germline variants in the Korean population are limited; however, the variant frequency is very low in the Korean Reference Genome Database (KRGDB), classifying it as a rare variant. Early diagnosis and proactive surveillance programs enable early treatment and contribute to improved prognosis.
variants.finding_summary: A pathogenic variant in the TP53 gene was identified in this specimen, which is likely to result in loss of p53 protein function. This variant is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome and significantly increases the risk of multiple cancer development. Comprehensive clinical management through genetic counseling and family history evaluation is recommended, and enrollment in a regular whole-body cancer screening program should be considered.

variants.treatment_strategies: For BRCA2 pathogenic variant carriers, risk-reducing mastectomy or enhanced surveillance (annual breast MRI + mammography starting at age 25) is recommended. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) is strongly recommended for ovarian cancer prevention at ages 35–40 or after completion of childbearing. For BRCA2 variant-related cancers, PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib) have received FDA approval, and sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy is high. For male carriers, prostate cancer screening (PSA + DRE) starting at age 40 and breast self-examination are recommended.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The general population frequency of BRCA2 pathogenic variants is estimated at approximately 1/400. BRCA2 variant frequency in Korean breast cancer patients is reported at approximately 3–5%, and Korean-specific founder mutations (e.g., c.7480C>T) are known. The lifetime breast cancer risk for Korean BRCA2 variant carriers has been reported at 49–57%, similar to that in Western populations. High response rates to PARP inhibitor therapy contribute to improved prognosis, and 5-year survival rates are significantly improved with early detection.
variants.finding_summary: A pathogenic variant in the BRCA2 gene was identified in this specimen, indicating impaired DNA double-strand break repair function. This corresponds to Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer syndrome (HBOC), with increased risks of breast cancer, ovarian cancer, prostate cancer, and pancreatic cancer. Genetic counseling is essential for establishing a risk management strategy, and cascade testing of family members is recommended.
associated_conditions: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome (HBOC)
variants.korean_specific_note: BRCA2 founder mutations (e.g., c.7480C>T) have been reported in the Korean population, and the Korean Hereditary Breast Cancer (KOHBRA) study has demonstrated that the Korean-specific variant spectrum differs from that in Western populations. In accordance with Korean National Cancer Screening Guidelines, BRCA2 variant carriers are classified as high-risk and are eligible for an enhanced screening program. Korean population frequency is approximately 3–5% among hereditary breast cancer patients vs approximately 1–2% in Western hereditary cancer cohorts.

variants.treatment_strategies: For cystic fibrosis, CFTR modulator therapy (ivacaftor, lumacaftor/ivacaftor, elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) is applied depending on the variant type. Trikafta (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) demonstrates particularly high efficacy for F508del variants, significantly improving FEV1. Inhaled antibiotics for pulmonary infection management, airway clearance therapy, and pancreatic enzyme supplementation are included in standard care. When a carrier is identified, testing the partner for CFTR variant carrier status is recommended to assess the child's risk of CF.
variants.frequency_prognosis: Cystic fibrosis occurs at a frequency of approximately 1/2,500 in Caucasians, making it the most common autosomal recessive disease in that population, with a carrier frequency of approximately 1/25. In contrast, it is extremely rare in East Asians including Koreans, estimated at approximately 1/90,000 to 1/350,000, with a carrier frequency of only approximately 1/150 to 1/300. The spectrum of CFTR variants reported in Koreans differs from that in Western populations, and the frequency of the F508del variant is markedly lower. The introduction of CFTR modulator therapy has greatly improved the median survival age of CF patients to over 50 years. Korean population frequency is approximately 1/90,000 vs 1/2,500 in Western (Caucasian) populations — approximately 36-fold lower.
variants.finding_summary: A pathogenic variant in the CFTR gene was identified in this specimen. The clinical significance of this variant depends on zygosity; biallelic variants are consistent with a diagnosis of cystic fibrosis. Since CF is an extremely rare disease in Koreans, comprehensive evaluation including clinical symptoms and sweat chloride testing is required. If the individual is a carrier, testing the partner is recommended to assess the child's risk of disease.
associated_conditions: Congenital Bilateral Absence of Vas Deferens (CBAVD)
variants.korean_specific_note: Cystic fibrosis is extremely rare in Koreans (approximately 1/90,000), approximately 36-fold lower in frequency than in Western populations. The spectrum of CFTR variants in Koreans includes many variants not covered by standard Western screening panels; therefore, whole-gene sequencing is recommended for diagnosis. Only a few dozen cases of CF have been reported in Korea, making delayed diagnosis common. Korean population frequency is approximately 1/90,000 vs 1/2,500 in Western populations.

variants.korean_flag: Very common variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

korean_pop.korean_flag: Very common variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

variants.treatment_strategies: When a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer is identified, antiplatelet therapy is switched from clopidogrel to prasugrel or ticagrelor; this is recommended at Level A evidence by CPIC and DPWG guidelines. When using proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), drug concentrations are elevated in PMs, so dose reduction of omeprazole or substitution with an alternative PPI (rabeprazole) is considered. When prescribing antidepressants (escitalopram, sertraline), the possibility of elevated blood concentrations in PMs warrants starting at a low dose and performing therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM). It is advisable to register pharmacogenomic results in the electronic medical record (EMR) so that automatic alerts are generated during future prescriptions.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The CYP2C19 *2 allele frequency in Koreans is approximately 25–29%, significantly higher than in Western populations (approximately 12–15%). The proportion of CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (PM) in Koreans is approximately 12–16%, roughly 5–6 times that of Western populations (approximately 2–5%). This results in a population-level significantly higher risk of clopidogrel hyporesponse in Korean patients; the Korean Society of Interventional Cardiology also recommended CYP2C19 testing in 2019. The CYP2C19 *3 allele is also present in Koreans at a frequency of approximately 5–10%, a loss-of-function variant specific to East Asians. Korean population frequency of poor metabolizers is 14% vs 2.5% in Western populations.
variants.finding_summary: A pharmacogenomic variant in the CYP2C19 gene was identified in this specimen. This variant directly affects the metabolism of numerous drugs including clopidogrel, and is a key determinant of drug responsiveness, particularly during antiplatelet therapy. The PM frequency in Koreans is approximately 5–6 times higher than in Western populations, making this clinically significant; genotype confirmation is recommended prior to prescribing these drugs.
variants.gene_full_name: Cytochrome P450 Family 2 Subfamily C Member 19
variants.korean_specific_note: The Korean CYP2C19 poor metabolizer frequency (approximately 14%) is approximately 5.6 times that of Western populations (approximately 2.5%), making clopidogrel hyporesponse a critically important clinical issue in the Korean population. A 2019 Korean Society of Interventional Cardiology consensus statement recommended CYP2C19 genotype testing for patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), and Korean National Health Insurance covers CYP2C19 genotype testing. Korean population frequency is 14% vs 2.5% in Western populations.

variants.korean_flag: Low frequency variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

korean_pop.korean_flag: Low frequency variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

variants.treatment_strategies: When HLA-B*5801 positive, allopurinol is absolutely contraindicated; febuxostat or benzbromarone is used as an alternative urate-lowering agent. Performing HLA-B*5801 genotype testing before prescribing urate-lowering agents in gout patients is recommended per CPIC and ACR (American College of Rheumatology) guidelines. When HLA-B*5701 positive, abacavir use is prohibited and an alternative antiretroviral agent is selected. In HLA-B*1502 positive individuals (Southeast Asian/East Asian ancestry), carbamazepine and related drugs (oxcarbazepine, phenytoin) are avoided.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The HLA-B*5801 allele frequency in Koreans is approximately 12%, which is 6–12 times higher than in European Caucasians (approximately 1–2%). This results in a population-level significantly higher risk of allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN in Korean gout patients. In Korea, HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol prescription has been covered by National Health Insurance since 2012, after which the incidence of allopurinol-related SJS/TEN has decreased. SJS/TEN is a severe adverse drug reaction with a mortality rate of 10–30%, and the cost-effectiveness of preventive genotype testing has been demonstrated. Korean population frequency of HLA-B*5801 is approximately 12% vs 1–2% in European populations.
variants.finding_summary: An HLA-B gene variant was identified in this specimen, which may be associated with an increased risk of severe adverse reactions to certain drugs. HLA-B*5801 carrier status must be confirmed before prescribing allopurinol, and accurate HLA typing is required. These variant results are for reference only; a separate confirmatory HLA typing test is recommended for clinical drug prescribing decisions.
variants.gene_full_name: Major Histocompatibility Complex, Class I, B
variants.korean_specific_note: The Korean HLA-B*5801 carrier rate of approximately 12% is much higher than in Europeans (1–2%), making this a high-risk population for allopurinol-induced severe adverse drug reactions. The Korean College of Rheumatology mandatorily recommends HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol prescription in gout patients, and National Health Insurance coverage applies. The HLA-B*1502 frequency in Koreans (approximately 1–6%) is also higher than in Europeans, requiring caution when prescribing carbamazepine. Korean population frequency of HLA-B*5801 is approximately 12% vs 1–2% in European populations.

variants.korean_flag: Low frequency variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

korean_pop.korean_flag: Low frequency variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

variants.treatment_strategies: For ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant carriers, annual breast MRI combined with mammography starting at age 40 is recommended for breast cancer risk management (NCCN guideline). The indication for risk-reducing mastectomy is determined based on individual family history and the functional impact of the variant. For cancers that arise, ATM-deficient tumors have been reported to have sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors, though the level of evidence is lower than for BRCA1/2. Radiation therapy is contraindicated in ataxia-telangiectasia patients due to the risk of excessive toxicity, and radiological imaging should also be minimized.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The general population carrier frequency of ATM pathogenic variants is estimated at approximately 1/100 to 1/200. The lifetime breast cancer risk for ATM heterozygous carriers is reported at approximately 15–40%, corresponding to moderate penetrance. Large-scale studies on the exact frequency of ATM variants in the Korean population are limited; however, ATM pathogenic variants are occasionally reported in Korean hereditary cancer panel testing. The incidence of ataxia-telangiectasia is approximately 1/40,000 to 1/100,000.
variants.finding_summary: A likely pathogenic variant in the ATM gene was identified in this specimen, associated with impaired DNA damage repair function. ATM heterozygous variants moderately increase the risks of breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, and prostate cancer. Risk assessment through genetic counseling and establishment of an appropriate cancer surveillance program is recommended.

variants.treatment_strategies: When MUTYH biallelic variants are confirmed (MAP), colonoscopic surveillance every 1–2 years starting at ages 18–20 is recommended; if adenomas are unmanageable, prophylactic colectomy is considered. Upper gastrointestinal surveillance by upper endoscopy every 3–5 years starting at ages 25–30 is recommended. For colorectal cancer, surgical resection is the primary treatment; since microsatellite stable (MSS) status predominates, standard chemotherapy regimens are applied. For monoallelic carriers, given a slightly elevated colorectal cancer risk compared to the general population, adherence to general colorectal cancer screening guidelines is recommended, with earlier screening considered based on family history.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The carrier (monoallelic) frequency of MUTYH pathogenic variants in Europeans is approximately 1–2%, with Y179C and G396D being the most common. The incidence of MAP (biallelic) is estimated at approximately 1/10,000 to 1/40,000. The frequency of MUTYH variants in Koreans has been reported to be lower than in Western populations, with a differing variant spectrum. The lifetime colorectal cancer risk in MAP patients is approximately 43–100%; appropriate surveillance and early intervention are critical for prognosis.
variants.finding_summary: A pathogenic variant in the MUTYH gene was identified in this specimen. The clinical significance differs greatly depending on zygosity (biallelic vs. monoallelic); biallelic variants require diagnosis of MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP) and a colorectal cancer surveillance program. Detailed family history evaluation and additional testing to confirm the variant status of the second allele are recommended.
variants.korean_specific_note: Reports of MUTYH variants in Koreans are fewer than in Western populations, but some Korean-specific variants (e.g., p.Q400R) have been reported. Y179C and G396D variants, which are common in Western populations, are rare in Koreans, requiring attention to ethnicity-specific variant interpretation.

variants.treatment_strategies: For PALB2 pathogenic variant carriers, annual breast MRI combined with mammography starting at age 30 is recommended (NCCN guideline), and risk-reducing mastectomy is also discussed individually. For PALB2 variant-related breast cancers, PARP inhibitors (olaparib) are effective in HR-deficient tumors; in 2023, olaparib FDA approval was expanded to PALB2 variant metastatic breast cancer. High sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy has been reported. Cascade genetic testing of family members is recommended.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The general population frequency of PALB2 pathogenic variants is estimated at approximately 1/1,000. The lifetime breast cancer risk for PALB2 variant carriers is approximately 35–60%, showing high penetrance comparable to BRCA2. PALB2 variants in Korean breast cancer patients are reported at a frequency of approximately 1–3%, and Korean-specific variants (e.g., c.2167_2168del) are known. Prognosis can be significantly improved through early detection and appropriate treatment.
variants.finding_summary: A pathogenic variant in the PALB2 gene was identified in this specimen, indicating impaired BRCA2-mediated DNA repair function. PALB2 is a key partner gene of BRCA2, significantly increasing the risks of breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, and ovarian cancer. An enhanced cancer surveillance program per NCCN guidelines and genetic counseling are recommended.
variants.korean_specific_note: In the Korean Hereditary Breast Cancer (KOHBRA) study, PALB2 was reported as the most important hereditary breast cancer gene after BRCA1/2, and a Korean-specific variant spectrum has been identified. Korean population frequency among hereditary breast cancer patients is approximately 1–3% vs approximately 0.6–1% in Western populations.

variants.treatment_strategies: Management of Noonan syndrome requires a multidisciplinary approach, including cardiac surgery or catheter procedures for congenital heart defects (pulmonary stenosis, etc.), growth hormone (GH) therapy (FDA approved), and hematological management for bleeding tendency. RAS-MAPK pathway inhibitors (MEK inhibitors: trametinib, selumetinib) are under investigation for Noonan syndrome-related hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and JMML, with significant efficacy reported in some patients. For JMML, hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is the only curative treatment, and MEK inhibitors are attempted for disease control prior to transplantation. Regular cardiac echocardiography, growth assessment, and developmental evaluation are required lifelong.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The incidence of Noonan syndrome is approximately 1/1,000 to 1/2,500, making it a relatively common genetic disease. PTPN11 germline variants account for approximately 50% of Noonan syndrome cases and most arise de novo. Noonan syndrome is also relatively frequently diagnosed in Korea, with PTPN11 variants accounting for the highest frequency. Prognosis is greatly influenced by the severity of cardiac disease, and with appropriate management, most patients can achieve a normal life expectancy.
variants.finding_summary: A likely pathogenic variant in the PTPN11 gene was identified in this specimen, which may result in gain-of-function of the SHP-2 protein leading to overactivation of the RAS-MAPK pathway. If germline, the association with Noonan syndrome should be evaluated; if somatic, the association with hematologic malignancies should be assessed. Genetic counseling incorporating clinical phenotype and family history is recommended.
associated_conditions: LEOPARD Syndrome (Noonan syndrome with multiple lentigines)

variants.korean_flag: Very common variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

korean_pop.korean_flag: Very common variant | Korean-specific variant (KRGDB only)

variants.treatment_strategies: Lifestyle interventions for Alzheimer's disease prevention are recommended in APOE e4 carriers (regular exercise, Mediterranean diet, cognitive stimulation activities, cardiovascular risk factor management). For cardiovascular risk management, regular lipid profile monitoring and statin therapy when necessary are considered, with observation of statin effects on cognitive function in APOE e4 carriers recommended. Anti-amyloid antibodies (lecanemab, donanemab) have been approved for early Alzheimer's disease treatment; however, APOE e4/e4 carriers have an elevated risk of ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities), requiring careful application. Type III hyperlipoproteinemia due to APOE e2/e2 is managed with dietary modification and fibrate-class medications.
variants.frequency_prognosis: The APOE e4 allele frequency in Koreans is approximately 8–11%, somewhat lower than in Europeans (approximately 14%). The proportion of Korean APOE e4 carriers (e3/e4 or e4/e4) is approximately 15–20%. The APOE e4 frequency in Korean Alzheimer's disease patients is approximately 40–50%, markedly higher than in the general population. The Alzheimer's disease risk-increasing effect of APOE e4 is consistently confirmed in Koreans as well, and the clinical importance of APOE genotype testing is growing in Korea's rapidly aging society. Korean population frequency of APOE e4 allele is approximately 8–11% vs approximately 14% in European populations.
variants.finding_summary: An APOE gene variant was identified in this specimen, which is a genetic factor related to lipid metabolism and Alzheimer's disease risk. Clinical significance is determined by APOE genotype (e2/e3/e4 combination); in particular, carrying the e4 allele requires management of increased Alzheimer's disease risk and cardiovascular disease risk. Individualized risk assessment and prevention strategy development through genetic counseling are recommended.
variants.korean_specific_note: The APOE e4 frequency in Koreans is somewhat lower than in Western populations, but the absolute number of Alzheimer's disease patients is rapidly increasing due to rapid population aging. Under the Korean National Dementia Responsibility Program, APOE genotype testing can be used as a reference for dementia risk assessment. Korean population frequency of APOE e4 allele is approximately 8–11% vs approximately 14% in European populations.
